questions.specification:
The figure summarizes synthetic biology experiments:
![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7hvefOYf2ibhIekX6RA7S.jpeg)

Bioinformatic analysis identified ADP/ATP carrier translocase proteins across plants, red algae, and endoparasites.

These genes were inserted into cyanobacteria (*Synechococcus elongatus*) to express ATP transporters.

Engineered cyanobacteria were then used in synthetic endosymbiosis with yeast mutants lacking functional mitochondria, allowing the photosynthetic bacteria to provide ATP directly to the yeast.

This mimics proposed mechanisms in the evolution of plastids and mitochondria, where endosymbionts contributed metabolic functions (like ATP production) to their host eukaryotic cells.

questions.specification:
Mitochondria are double-membraned organelles responsible for producing most of the cell's ATP through aerobic respiration. The inner mitochondrial membrane (IMM) contains proteins like ATP synthase and the respiratory chain complexes. The mitochondrial matrix (MM) houses enzymes involved in the Krebs cycle (TCA cycle), and mitochondria have their own DNA ($mtDNA$). Fatty acids and glucose can both contribute to $ATP$ production.

Disruption in mitochondrial structure or function can lead to reduced energy production and the activation of cell death pathways.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/qKgozqQ5KkQ7WoctxMW2c.jpeg)

Mitochondrial structure and metabolism

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/KHqqLyGhQVuFMq6MkuFKy.jpeg)

Consequences of mitochondrial dysfunction

parts.markscheme:

- **Cristae / folded IMM**: increases surface area for attachment of respiratory chain complexes and $ATP$ synthase, increasing capacity for $ATP$ production <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: State the two major energy sources that enter the mitochondria for $ATP$ production, based on Figure a.
parts.markscheme:

parts.content: Cancer cells are shown to favor lactate production in Figure a. Explain why this results in less efficient $ATP$ production than aerobic respiration.
parts.markscheme:
- Lactate production is associated with anaerobic glycolysis, which yields a much lower net $ATP$ per glucose (e.g. 2 $ATP$) than aerobic respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Aerobic respiration uses the TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation, producing much more $ATP$ per glucose <marks>1 mark</marks>

Additional acceptable points (do not award beyond max):
- Conversion of pyruvate to lactate regenerates NAD$^+$, allowing glycolysis to continue but bypasses oxidative phosphorylation
- Less energy is extracted because glucose is not fully oxidized to CO$_2$ (energy remains in lactate)

parts.content: With reference to Figure b, suggest one reason why cells have multiple ways of responding to mitochondrial dysfunction.

parts.content: Outline one role of mitochondrial DNA ($mtDNA$) and explain how its damage could affect cell function.

parts.markscheme:
- The pump is an integral transmembrane protein with high affinity for sodium ions in its internal configuration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Three sodium ions ($$3Na^+$$) bind to specific binding sites on the intracellular side of the pump <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The binding of $$Na^+$$ triggers the hydrolysis of $$ATP$$, which phosphorylates the pump (attaches a phosphate group) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Phosphorylation causes a conformational change (shape change) in the protein, translocating the $$Na^+$$ across the membrane <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The $$3Na^+$$ ions are released to the extracellular fluid, and the pump now has a high affinity for potassium ions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Two potassium ions ($$2K^+$$) bind to the pump from the extracellular side, triggering the release of the phosphate group <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Dephosphorylation causes the pump to return to its original conformation, releasing the $$2K^+$$ into the cytoplasm <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Because more positive charge is moved out ($$3Na^+$$) than in ($$2K^+$$), the pump is electrogenic and establishes a negative internal membrane potential <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award maximum 7 marks</note>

parts.content: Explain how the fluidity of the phospholipid bilayer enables the processes of endocytosis and exocytosis.
parts.markscheme:
- The fluid nature of the membrane is due to the weak hydrophobic interactions between the fatty acid tails, allowing phospholipids to move laterally <marks>1 mark</marks>
- This flexibility allows the plasma membrane to change shape and invaginate to form vesicles during endocytosis <marks>1 mark</marks>
- During exocytosis, the phospholipid bilayers of the secretory vesicle and the plasma membrane can merge/fuse because they are both fluid <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fusion is followed by the spontaneous rearrangement of the bilayer to become continuous, releasing the vesicle's contents <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Fluidity ensures that the membrane can break and reform (re-seal) without losing its integrity or leaking cytoplasm <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award maximum 4 marks</note>

parts.content: Using the diagram of a eukaryotic cell, explain how the structure and function of the rough ER and Golgi apparatus are related.
parts.markscheme:
For rough ER:
- Ribosomes attached to outer membrane surface enables protein synthesis directly into ER lumen <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Extensive membrane network provides large surface area for protein synthesis and processing <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Connection to nuclear envelope allows efficient transfer of information from nucleus to site of protein synthesis <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Continuous internal space (lumen) allows newly synthesized proteins to undergo folding/modification <marks>1 mark</marks>

For Golgi apparatus:
- Stack of flattened cisternae provides compartmentalization for sequential protein modifications <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Distinct cis and trans faces enable directional protein processing <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Membrane-bound structure allows concentration of modifying enzymes for efficient protein processing <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Vesicle formation at trans face enables sorted proteins to be packaged for specific destinations <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Each marking point requires explicit linking of a structural feature to its functional significance</note>

steps.title: Analyze the Rough ER: Ribosomes
steps.content: The **rough endoplasmic reticulum (rER)** is characterized by having **ribosomes** attached to its outer membrane surface. This structural feature is essential for its function: it allows proteins to be synthesized (translated) directly into the **ER lumen** (the internal space). This ensures that proteins destined for secretion or membrane insertion are kept separate from the cytoplasm right from the start.

steps.title: Analyze the Rough ER: Lumen
steps.content: The rER consists of an extensive network of membrane-bound sacs. This provides a **large surface area** for ribosomes to attach, maximizing protein production. Inside, the **continuous internal lumen** provides a controlled environment where newly synthesized proteins can undergo essential **folding and initial modifications** (like adding carbohydrate chains) before being transported away.

steps.title: Analyze the Rough ER: Connectivity
steps.content: Structurally, the rER is often **continuous with the nuclear envelope**. This proximity is functional: it allows for the efficient transfer of genetic information (mRNA) from the nucleus directly to the site of protein synthesis, streamlining the gene expression process.

steps.title: Analyze the Golgi: Cisternae
steps.content: Moving to the **Golgi apparatus**, its structure consists of a stack of flattened, membrane-bound sacs called **cisternae**. Unlike the rER, these are usually not physically connected to each other. This compartmentalization allows the Golgi to contain different **modifying enzymes** in each sac, enabling the **sequential modification** of proteins as they move through the stack.

steps.title: Analyze the Golgi: Polarity
steps.content: The Golgi apparatus has a distinct **polarity**, with a **cis face** (receiving side) and a **trans face** (shipping side). This structural arrangement ensures **directional processing**: vesicles from the rER fuse with the cis face, and after being processed, the finished products exit from the trans face.

steps.title: Analyze the Golgi: Vesicles
steps.content: A key functional feature of the Golgi is its ability to form **vesicles** at the trans face. This allows the organelle to **sort and package** proteins into specialized containers. These vesicles then carry the proteins to specific destinations, such as the plasma membrane for secretion (exocytosis) or to become lysosomes.

steps.title: Synthesize the Relationship
steps.content: The relationship is a functional relay: the rER focuses on the **synthesis and folding** of proteins, which are then transported via transport vesicles to the Golgi. The Golgi then takes over to **refine, sort, and distribute** these proteins. This collaboration is a core part of the secretory pathway discussed in **Topic B2.2: Organelles and compartmentalization**.

steps.title: The Endomembrane System Journey
steps.content:
To answer this question effectively, we need to trace the "journey" of a protein. The **rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (rER)** and the **Golgi apparatus** are the two primary stops in the endomembrane system. Their structures are specialized to ensure proteins are built, folded, modified, and shipped to the correct destination. 

This process is covered in **Topic B2.2: Organelles and compartmentalization**. Understanding the relationship between these two organelles is key to explaining how eukaryotic cells manage complex secretions.

highlights.text: explain how the structure and function of the rough ER and Golgi apparatus are related

steps.title: Synthesis at the rER
steps.content:
The journey begins on the surface of the **rough ER**. Its "rough" appearance comes from **ribosomes** attached to the outer membrane surface. This structural feature allows proteins to be synthesized directly into the **ER lumen** (the internal space). 

Additionally, the rER membrane is often continuous with the **nuclear envelope**, which facilitates the efficient transfer of genetic information (mRNA) from the nucleus to the site of protein synthesis.

highlights.text: Ribosomes attached to outer membrane surface enables protein synthesis directly into ER lumen

steps.title: Processing in the Lumen
steps.content: The rER is an extensive network of folded membranes. This provides a **large surface area** for ribosomes to attach, maximizing protein production. Once inside the **lumen**, the environment is specialized to allow newly synthesized proteins to undergo their initial **folding** and chemical modifications (like adding carbohydrate chains to form glycoproteins).

highlights.text: Continuous internal space (lumen) allows newly synthesized proteins to undergo folding/modification

steps.title: Transport to the Golgi
steps.content: Once the initial processing is done in the rER, the proteins are packaged into **transport vesicles**. These membrane-bound bubbles bud off the rER and carry the proteins to the "receiving" side of the Golgi apparatus. This highlights the functional link: the rER creates the raw product, and vesicles provide the bridge to the next workstation.

steps.title: Sequential Modification in Golgi
steps.content:
The **Golgi apparatus** consists of a stack of flattened membrane sacs called **cisternae**. This structure provides distinct **compartments**, which is crucial because protein modification happens in stages. 

By keeping different enzymes in different cisternae, the cell can ensure that modifications (like trimming sugars or adding phosphate groups) occur in a specific, **sequential order**.

highlights.text: Stack of flattened cisternae provides compartmentalization for sequential protein modifications

steps.title: Directionality and Faces
steps.content:
The Golgi has a clear polarity with two distinct sides:
- The **cis face**: The "receiving" side that faces the rER.
- The **trans face**: The "shipping" side that faces the plasma membrane.

This structural polarity ensures **directional protein processing**, preventing proteins from moving backward and ensuring they reach the final assembly stage correctly.

highlights.text: Distinct cis and trans faces enable directional protein processing

steps.title: Sorting and Final Export
steps.content:
Finally, at the **trans face**, the Golgi performs its "post office" function. It sorts the processed proteins and packages them into **secretory vesicles**. 

The structure of the trans face allows it to bud off these vesicles, which then travel to specific destinations, such as the cell membrane for **exocytosis** or to become lysosomes within the cell.

highlights.text: Vesicle formation at trans face enables sorted proteins to be packaged for specific destinations
